Brother Hickey,

reference: BROTHER MAC ARDLE BROTHER KEOGH
transcript: Brother Mc Cardle Brother Kieff

transcript: Their piety would be like their names, like their faces, like their clothes and it was idle for him to tell himself that their humble and contrite hearts, it might be, paid a far richer tribute of devotion than his had ever been a gift tenfold more acceptable than his elaborate adoration.

transcript: By being studious of colour they are studious of division and while the chiaroscuroist devotes himself to the representation of degrees of force in one thing, unseparated light, the colourist has for his function the attainment of beauty by arrangement of the divisions of light.

reference: FOR BELIEVE ME THE FINAL PHILOSOPHY OF ART CAN ONLY RATIFY THEIR OPINION THAT THE BEAUTY OF A COCK ROBIN IS TO BE RED AND OF A GRASS PLOT TO BE GREEN AND THE BEST SKILL OF ART IS IN INSTANTLY SEIZING ON THE MANIFOLD DELICIOUSNESS OF LIGHT WHICH YOU CAN ONLY SEIZE BY PRECISION OF INSTANTANEOUS TOUCH
transcript: For, believe me, the final philosophy of art can only ratify their opinion that the beauty of a cock robin is to be red, and of a grass plot to be green; and the best skill of art is in instantly seizing on the manifold deliciousness of light, which you can only seize by precision of instantaneous touch.

reference: NOW YOU WILL SEE IN THESE STUDIES THAT THE MOMENT THE WHITE IS INCLOSED PROPERLY AND HARMONIZED WITH THE OTHER HUES IT BECOMES SOMEHOW MORE PRECIOUS AND PEARLY THAN THE WHITE PAPER AND THAT I AM NOT AFRAID TO LEAVE A WHOLE FIELD OF UNTREATED WHITE PAPER ALL ROUND IT BEING SURE THAT EVEN THE LITTLE DIAMONDS IN THE ROUND WINDOW WILL TELL AS JEWELS IF THEY ARE GRADATED JUSTLY
transcript: Now you will see in these studies that the moment the white is enclosed properly and harmonized with the other hues, it becomes somehow more precious and pearly than the white paper, and that I am not afraid to leave a whole field of untreated white paper all round it, being sure that even the little diamonds in the round window will tell as jewels if they are gradated justly.

reference: BUT IN THIS VIGNETTE COPIED FROM TURNER YOU HAVE THE TWO PRINCIPLES BROUGHT OUT PERFECTLY
transcript: But in this vignette, copied from Turner, you have the two principles brought out perfectly.

reference: THEY ARE BEYOND ALL OTHER WORKS THAT I KNOW EXISTING DEPENDENT FOR THEIR EFFECT ON LOW SUBDUED TONES THEIR FAVORITE CHOICE IN TIME OF DAY BEING EITHER DAWN OR TWILIGHT AND EVEN THEIR BRIGHTEST SUNSETS PRODUCED CHIEFLY OUT OF GRAY PAPER
transcript: They are beyond all other works that I know existing, dependent for their effect on low, subdued tones their favorite choice in time of day being either dawn or twilight, and even their brightest sunsets produced chiefly out of gray paper.

reference: IT MAY BE THAT A GREAT COLORIST WILL USE HIS UTMOST FORCE OF COLOR AS A SINGER HIS FULL POWER OF VOICE BUT LOUD OR LOW THE VIRTUE IS IN BOTH CASES ALWAYS IN REFINEMENT NEVER IN LOUDNESS
transcript: It may be that a great colorist will use his utmost force of color, as a singer his full power of voice but, loud or low, the virtue is in both cases always in refinement, never in loudness.

reference: IT MUST REMEMBER BE ONE OR THE OTHER
transcript: It must, remember, be one or the other.

reference: DO NOT THEREFORE THINK THAT THE GOTHIC SCHOOL IS AN EASY ONE
transcript: Do not, therefore, think that the Gothic school is an easy one.

reference: THE LAW OF THAT SCHOOL IS THAT EVERYTHING SHALL BE SEEN CLEARLY OR AT LEAST ONLY IN SUCH MIST OR FAINTNESS AS SHALL BE DELIGHTFUL AND I HAVE NO DOUBT THAT THE BEST INTRODUCTION TO IT WOULD BE THE ELEMENTARY PRACTICE OF PAINTING EVERY STUDY ON A GOLDEN GROUND
transcript: The law of that school is that everything shall be seen clearly, or at least only in such mist or faintness as shall be delightful. And I have no doubt that the best introduction to it would be the elementary practice of painting every study on a golden ground.

reference: THIS AT ONCE COMPELS YOU TO UNDERSTAND THAT THE WORK IS TO BE IMAGINATIVE AND DECORATIVE THAT IT REPRESENTS BEAUTIFUL THINGS IN THE CLEAREST WAY BUT NOT UNDER EXISTING CONDITIONS AND THAT IN FACT YOU ARE PRODUCING JEWELER'S WORK RATHER THAN PICTURES
transcript: This at once compels you to understand that the work is to be imaginative and decorative, that it represents beautiful things in the clearest way, but not under existing conditions, and that, in fact, you are producing jeweller's work rather than pictures.

reference: THAT A STYLE IS RESTRAINED OR SEVERE DOES NOT MEAN THAT IT IS ALSO ERRONEOUS
transcript: That a style is restrained or severe does not mean that it is also erroneous.

reference: IN ALL EARLY GOTHIC ART INDEED YOU WILL FIND FAILURE OF THIS KIND ESPECIALLY DISTORTION AND RIGIDITY WHICH ARE IN MANY RESPECTS PAINFULLY TO BE COMPARED WITH THE SPLENDID REPOSE OF CLASSIC ART
transcript: In all early Gothic art, indeed, you will find failure of this kind, especially distortion and rigidity, which are in many respects painfully to be compared with the splendid repose of classic art.

reference: THE LARGE LETTER CONTAINS INDEED ENTIRELY FEEBLE AND ILL DRAWN FIGURES THAT IS MERELY CHILDISH AND FAILING WORK OF AN INFERIOR HAND IT IS NOT CHARACTERISTIC OF GOTHIC OR ANY OTHER SCHOOL
transcript: The large letter contains, indeed, entirely feeble and ill drawn figures. That is merely childish and failing work of an inferior hand it is not characteristic of Gothic or any other school.

reference: BUT OBSERVE YOU CAN ONLY DO THIS ON ONE CONDITION THAT OF STRIVING ALSO TO CREATE IN REALITY THE BEAUTY WHICH YOU SEEK IN IMAGINATION
transcript: But, observe, you can only do this on one condition, that of striving also to create in reality the beauty which you seek in imagination.

reference: IT WILL BE WHOLLY IMPOSSIBLE FOR YOU TO RETAIN THE TRANQUILLITY OF TEMPER AND FELICITY OF FAITH NECESSARY FOR NOBLE PURIST PAINTING UNLESS YOU ARE ACTIVELY ENGAGED IN PROMOTING THE FELICITY AND PEACE OF PRACTICAL LIFE
transcript: It will be wholly impossible for you to retain the tranquillity of temper and felicity of faith necessary for noble purist painting, unless you are actively engaged in promoting the felicity and peace of practical life.

reference: YOU MUST LOOK AT HIM IN THE FACE FIGHT HIM CONQUER HIM WITH WHAT SCATHE YOU MAY YOU NEED NOT THINK TO KEEP OUT OF THE WAY OF HIM
transcript: You must look him in the face, fight him, conquer him, with what scathe you may. You need not think to keep out of the way of him.

reference: THE COLORIST SAYS FIRST OF ALL AS MY DELICIOUS PAROQUET WAS RUBY SO THIS NASTY VIPER SHALL BE BLACK AND THEN IS THE QUESTION CAN I ROUND HIM OFF EVEN THOUGH HE IS BLACK AND MAKE HIM SLIMY AND YET SPRINGY AND CLOSE DOWN CLOTTED LIKE A POOL OF BLACK BLOOD ON THE EARTH ALL THE SAME
transcript: The colorist says, First of all, as my delicious perruque was ruby, so this nasty viper shall be black. And then is the question, Can I round him off, even though he is black, and make him slimy, and yet springy and close down, clotted like a pool of black blood on the earth, all the same?

reference: NOTHING WILL BE MORE PRECIOUS TO YOU I THINK IN THE PRACTICAL STUDY OF ART THAN THE CONVICTION WHICH WILL FORCE ITSELF ON YOU MORE AND MORE EVERY HOUR OF THE WAY ALL THINGS ARE BOUND TOGETHER LITTLE AND GREAT IN SPIRIT AND IN MATTER
transcript: Nothing will be more precious to you, I think, in the practical study of art, than the conviction, which will force itself on you more and more every hour, of the way all things are bound together, little and great, in spirit and in matter.

reference: YOU KNOW I HAVE JUST BEEN TELLING YOU HOW THIS SCHOOL OF MATERIALISM AND CLAY INVOLVED ITSELF AT LAST IN CLOUD AND FIRE
transcript: You know I have just been telling you how this school of materialism in clay involved itself at last in cloud and fire.

reference: HERE IS AN EQUALLY TYPICAL GREEK SCHOOL LANDSCAPE BY WILSON LOST WHOLLY IN GOLDEN MIST THE TREES SO SLIGHTLY DRAWN THAT YOU DON'T KNOW IF THEY ARE TREES OR TOWERS AND NO CARE FOR COLOR WHATEVER PERFECTLY DECEPTIVE AND MARVELOUS EFFECT OF SUNSHINE THROUGH THE MIST APOLLO AND THE PYTHON
transcript: Here is an equally typical Greek school landscape by Wilson, lost wholly in golden mist, the trees so slightly drawn that you don't know if they are trees or towers, and no care for colour whatsoever. Perfectly deceptive and marvellous effect of sunshine through the mist. Apollo and the Python.

reference: NOW HERE IS RAPHAEL EXACTLY BETWEEN THE TWO TREES STILL DRAWN LEAF BY LEAF WHOLLY FORMAL BUT BEAUTIFUL MIST COMING GRADUALLY INTO THE DISTANCE
transcript: Now here is Raphael exactly between the two, trees still drawn leaf by leaf, wholly formal, but beautiful mist coming gradually into the distance.

reference: WELL THEN LAST HERE IS TURNER'S GREEK SCHOOL OF THE HIGHEST CLASS AND YOU DEFINE HIS ART ABSOLUTELY AS FIRST THE DISPLAYING INTENSELY AND WITH THE STERNEST INTELLECT OF NATURAL FORM AS IT IS AND THEN THE ENVELOPMENT OF IT WITH CLOUD AND FIRE
transcript: Well, then, last, here is Turner's Greek school of the highest class. And you define his art absolutely as first the displaying intensely and with the sternest intellect of natural form as it is, and then the envelopment of it with cloud and fire.

reference: ONLY THERE ARE TWO SORTS OF CLOUD AND FIRE
transcript: Only there are two sorts of cloud and fire.

reference: HE KNOWS THEM BOTH
transcript: He knows them both.

transcript: There's one and there's another, the Dudley and the Flint.

transcript: It is only a pencil outline by Edward Burne-Jones, in illustration of the story of Psyche. It is the introduction of Psyche, after all her troubles, into heaven.

reference: EXQUISITE ORDER AND UNIVERSAL WITH ETERNAL LIFE AND LIGHT THIS IS THE FAITH AND EFFORT OF THE SCHOOLS OF CRYSTAL AND YOU MAY DESCRIBE AND COMPLETE THEIR WORK QUITE LITERALLY BY TAKING ANY VERSES OF CHAUCER IN HIS TENDER MOOD AND OBSERVING HOW HE INSISTS ON THE CLEARNESS AND BRIGHTNESS FIRST AND THEN ON THE ORDER
transcript: Exquisite order and universal, with eternal life and light this is the faith and effort of the schools of crystal. reference: THUS IN CHAUCER'S DREAM

transcript: I don't think, mister Hale, you have done quite right in introducing such a person to us without telling us what he had been.

transcript: I really was very much afraid of showing him how much shocked I was at some parts of what he said.

transcript: His father speculated wildly, failed, and then killed himself because he could not bear the disgrace.

transcript: All his former friends shrunk from the disclosures that had to be made of his dishonest gambling, wild, hopeless struggles made with other people's money to regain his own moderate portion of wealth.

transcript: No one came forwards to help the mother and this boy.

transcript: At least, no friend came forwards immediately, and mister Thornton is not one, I fancy, to wait till tardy kindness comes to find her out.

reference: SO THEY LEFT MILTON

transcript: When he spoke of the mechanical powers, he evidently looked upon them only as new ways of extending trade and making money.

transcript: and the poor men around him. They were poor because they were vicious, out of the pale of his sympathies, because they had not his iron nature, and the capabilities that it gives him for being rich.

transcript: Margaret was collecting her mother's working materials and preparing to go to bed.

transcript: Papa, I do think mister Thornton a very remarkable man but personally I don't like him at all.

transcript: She and Dixon held mysterious consultations in her bedroom, from which Dixon would come out crying and cross, as was her custom when any distress of her mistress called upon her sympathy.

transcript: But though she received caresses and fond words back again, in such profusion as would have gladdened her formerly, yet she felt that there was a secret withheld from her, and she believed it bore serious reference to her mother's health.

transcript: She lay awake very long this night, planning how to lessen the evil influence of the Milton life on her mother.

transcript: A servant to give Dixon permanent assistance should be got, if she gave up the whole time to the search and then, at any rate, her mother might have all the personal attentions she required, and had been accustomed to her whole life.

transcript: Visiting register offices, seeing all manner of unlikely people, and very few in the least likely, absorbed Margaret's time and thoughts for several days.

transcript: Margaret turned round to walk alongside of the girl in her feeble progress homeward.

transcript: At last she said in a low voice,

transcript: Bessy was silent in her turn for a minute or two. Then she replied

transcript: You know, I'm a stranger here, so perhaps I'm not so quick at understanding what you mean as if I'd lived all my life in Milton.

transcript: Take notice that this is not my kind of haughtiness, papa, if I have any at all, which I don't agree to, though you're always accusing me of it.

transcript: I don't know positively that it is hers either, but from little things I have gathered from him I fancy so.

transcript: They cared too little to ask in what manner her son had spoken about her,

reference: IF CALLED TO A CASE SUPPOSED OR SUSPECTED TO BE ONE OF POISONING THE MEDICAL MAN HAS TWO DUTIES TO PERFORM TO SAVE THE PATIENT'S LIFE AND TO PLACE HIMSELF IN A POSITION TO GIVE EVIDENCE IF CALLED ON TO DO SO
transcript: If called to a case supposed or suspected to be one of poisoning, the medical man has two duties to perform to save the patient's life, and to place himself in a position to give evidence if called on to do so.

reference: HE SHOULD MAKE INQUIRIES AS TO SYMPTOMS AND TIME AT WHICH FOOD OR MEDICINE WAS LAST TAKEN
transcript: he should make inquiries as to symptoms, and time at which food or medicine was last taken.

reference: HE SHOULD NOTICE THE POSITION AND TEMPERATURE OF THE BODY THE CONDITION OF RIGOR MORTIS MARKS OF VIOLENCE APPEARANCE OF LIPS AND MOUTH
transcript: He should notice the position and temperature of the body, the condition of rigor mortis, marks of violence, appearance of lips and mouth.

reference: IN MAKING A POST MORTEM EXAMINATION THE ALIMENTARY CANAL SHOULD BE REMOVED AND PRESERVED FOR FURTHER INVESTIGATION
transcript: In making a post mortem examination, the alimentary canal should be removed and preserved for further investigation.

reference: THE GUT AND THE GULLET BEING CUT ACROSS BETWEEN THESE LIGATURES THE STOMACH MAY BE REMOVED ENTIRE WITHOUT SPILLING ITS CONTENTS
transcript: The gut and the gullet being cut across between these ligatures, the stomach may be removed entire without spilling its contents.

reference: IF THE MEDICAL PRACTITIONER IS IN DOUBT ON ANY POINT HE SHOULD OBTAIN TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE FROM SOMEONE WHO HAS PAID ATTENTION TO THE SUBJECT
transcript: If the medical practitioner is in doubt on any point, he should obtain technical assistance from someone who has paid attention to the subject.

reference: IN A CASE OF ATTEMPTED SUICIDE BY POISONING IS IT THE DUTY OF THE DOCTOR TO INFORM THE POLICE
transcript: In a case of attempted suicide by poisoning, is it the duty of the doctor to inform the police?

reference: THE BEST EMETIC IS THAT WHICH IS AT HAND
transcript: The best emetic is that which is at hand.

reference: THE DOSE FOR AN ADULT IS TEN MINIMS
transcript: The dose for an adult is ten minims.

reference: APOMORPHINE IS NOT ALLIED IN PHYSIOLOGICAL ACTION TO MORPHINE AND MAY BE GIVEN IN CASES OF NARCOTIC POISONING
transcript: Ephemerphine is not allied in physiological action to morphine, and may be given in cases of narcotic poisoning.

reference: TICKLING THE FAUCES WITH A FEATHER MAY EXCITE VOMITING
transcript: Tickling the fauces with a feather may excite vomiting.

reference: IN USING THE ELASTIC STOMACH TUBE SOME FLUID SHOULD BE INTRODUCED INTO THE STOMACH BEFORE ATTEMPTING TO EMPTY IT OR A PORTION OF THE MUCOUS MEMBRANE MAY BE SUCKED INTO THE APERTURE
transcript: In using the elastic stomach tube, some fluid should be introduced into the stomach before attempting to empty it, or a portion of the mucous membrane may be sucked into the aperture.

reference: THE TUBE SHOULD BE EXAMINED TO SEE THAT IT IS NOT BROKEN OR CRACKED AS ACCIDENTS HAVE HAPPENED FROM NEGLECTING THIS PRECAUTION
transcript: The tube should be examined to see that it is not broken or cracked, as accidents have happened from neglecting this precaution.

reference: ANTIDOTES ARE USUALLY GIVEN HYPODERMICALLY OR IF BY MOUTH IN THE FORM OF TABLETS
transcript: Antidotes are usually given hypodermically, or, if by mouth, in the form of tablets.

reference: IN THE ABSENCE OF A HYPODERMIC SYRINGE THE REMEDY MAY BE GIVEN BY THE RECTUM
transcript: In the absence of a hypodermic syringe, the remedy may be given by the rectum.

reference: NOTICE THE SMELL COLOUR AND GENERAL APPEARANCE OF THE MATTER SUBMITTED FOR EXAMINATION
transcript: Notice the smell, color, and general appearance of the matter submitted for examination.

reference: FOR THE SEPARATION OF AN ALKALOID THE FOLLOWING IS THE PROCESS OF STAS OTTO
transcript: For the separation of an alkaloid the following is the process of Stahl's Otto

reference: THIS PROCESS IS BASED UPON THE PRINCIPLE THAT THE SALTS OF THE ALKALOIDS ARE SOLUBLE IN ALCOHOL AND WATER AND INSOLUBLE IN ETHER
transcript: This process is based upon the principle that the salts of the alkaloids are soluble in alcohol and water and insoluble in ether.

reference: THE PURE ALKALOIDS WITH THE EXCEPTION OF MORPHINE IN ITS CRYSTALLINE FORM ARE SOLUBLE IN ETHER
transcript: The pure alkaloids, with the exception of morphine in its crystalline form, are soluble in ether.

reference: TWO COOL THE MIXTURE AND FILTER WASH THE RESIDUE WITH STRONG ALCOHOL AND MIX THE FILTRATES
transcript: two. Cool the mixture and filter. Wash the residue with strong alcohol and mix the filtrates.

reference: THE RESIDUE MAY BE SET ASIDE FOR THE DETECTION OF THE METALLIC POISONS IF SUSPECTED EXPEL THE ALCOHOL BY CAREFUL EVAPORATION
transcript: The residue may be set aside for the detection of the metallic poisons, if suspected. Expel the alcohol by careful evaporation.

reference: ON THE EVAPORATION OF THE ALCOHOL THE RESINOUS AND FATTY MATTERS SEPARATE
transcript: On the evaporation of the alcohol, the resinous and fatty matters separate.

reference: EVAPORATE THE FILTRATE TO A SYRUP AND EXTRACT WITH SUCCESSIVE PORTIONS OF ABSOLUTE ALCOHOL
transcript: Evaporate the filtrate to a syrup, and extract with successive portions of absolute alcohol.

reference: SEPARATE THE ETHEREAL SOLUTION AND EVAPORATE
transcript: Separate the ethereal solution and evaporate.

reference: FIVE A PART OF THIS ETHEREAL SOLUTION IS POURED INTO A WATCH GLASS AND ALLOWED TO EVAPORATE
transcript: five. A part of this ethereal solution is poured into a watch glass and allowed to evaporate.

reference: TO PURIFY IT ADD A SMALL QUANTITY OF DILUTE SULPHURIC ACID AND AFTER EVAPORATING TO THREE QUARTERS OF ITS BULK ADD A SATURATED SOLUTION OF CARBONATE OF POTASH OR SODA
transcript: To purify it, add a small quantity of dilute sulphuric acid, and, after evaporating to three quarters of its bulk, add a saturated solution of carbonate of potash or soda.

reference: BOIL THE FINELY DIVIDED SUBSTANCE WITH ABOUT ONE EIGHTH ITS BULK OF PURE HYDROCHLORIC ACID ADD FROM TIME TO TIME POTASSIC CHLORATE UNTIL THE SOLIDS ARE REDUCED TO A STRAW YELLOW FLUID
transcript: Boil the finely divided substance with about one eighth its bulk of pure hydrochloric acid. Add from time to time potassic chlorate until the solids are reduced to a straw yellow fluid.

reference: THE RESIDUE OF THE MATERIAL AFTER DIGESTION WITH HYDROCHLORIC ACID AND POTASSIUM CHLORATE MAY HAVE TO BE EXAMINED FOR SILVER LEAD AND BARIUM
transcript: The residue of the material after digestion with hydrochloric acid and potassium chlorate may have to be examined for silver, lead, and barium.
